verses.text: Jacob gathers his sons: come together, that I may tell you what shall befall you in the last days. The blessing is also prophecy. Listen, ye sons of Jacob, hearken unto Israel your father. He uses both names — what he was, and who he is now.

Jacob gathers his sons: come together, that I may tell you what shall befall you in the last days. The blessing is also prophecy. Listen, ye sons of Jacob, hearken unto Israel your father. He uses both names — what he was, and who he is now.

Reuben, you are my firstborn, my might, the beginning of my strength — excellency of dignity and power. Unstable as water, you shall not excel. Because you went up to your father's bed — you defiled it. The oldest son's sin is named without flinching.

Reuben, you are my firstborn, my might, the beginning of my strength — excellency of dignity and power. Unstable as water, you shall not excel. Because you went up to your father's bed — you defiled it. The oldest son's sin is named without flinching.

Simeon and Levi are brothers — instruments of cruelty are in their dwellings. O my soul, come not into their secret. In their anger they killed a man; in their wrath they hamstrung an ox. Cursed be their anger, for it was fierce. I will divide them in Jacob and scatter them in Israel. The Shechem massacre comes back to mind, generations later.

Simeon and Levi are brothers — instruments of cruelty are in their dwellings. O my soul, come not into their secret. In their anger they killed a man; in their wrath they hamstrung an ox. Cursed be their anger, for it was fierce. I will divide them in Jacob and scatter them in Israel. The Shechem massacre comes back to mind, generations later.

Simeon and Levi are brothers — instruments of cruelty are in their dwellings. O my soul, come not into their secret. In their anger they killed a man; in their wrath they hamstrung an ox. Cursed be their anger, for it was fierce. I will divide them in Jacob and scatter them in Israel. The Shechem massacre comes back to mind, generations later.

Judah, your brothers shall praise you — your hand on the neck of your enemies; your father's children shall bow down to you. Judah is a lion's whelp; from the prey, my son, you have gone up. He stooped, couched as a lion — who shall rouse him up? The brother who once couldn't sell off Joseph is now blessed with kingly imagery.

Judah, your brothers shall praise you — your hand on the neck of your enemies; your father's children shall bow down to you. Judah is a lion's whelp; from the prey, my son, you have gone up. He stooped, couched as a lion — who shall rouse him up? The brother who once couldn't sell off Joseph is now blessed with kingly imagery.

The sceptre shall not depart from Judah, nor a lawgiver from between his feet, until Shiloh comes — and to him shall the gathering of the people be. Binding his foal to the vine; he washes garments in wine. His eyes red with wine, teeth white with milk. The Messiah prophecy lands in this tribe's stanza.

The sceptre shall not depart from Judah, nor a lawgiver from between his feet, until Shiloh comes — and to him shall the gathering of the people be. Binding his foal to the vine; he washes garments in wine. His eyes red with wine, teeth white with milk. The Messiah prophecy lands in this tribe's stanza.

The sceptre shall not depart from Judah, nor a lawgiver from between his feet, until Shiloh comes — and to him shall the gathering of the people be. Binding his foal to the vine; he washes garments in wine. His eyes red with wine, teeth white with milk. The Messiah prophecy lands in this tribe's stanza.
Zebulun shall dwell by the haven of the sea; he shall be a haven for ships; his border shall reach to Sidon. Each tribe gets a geography — even before they have one.

Issachar is a strong donkey, couching between two burdens. He saw rest was good and the land pleasant — and bowed his shoulder to bear, and became a servant unto tribute. Comfort can quietly cost a tribe its independence.

Issachar is a strong donkey, couching between two burdens. He saw rest was good and the land pleasant — and bowed his shoulder to bear, and became a servant unto tribute. Comfort can quietly cost a tribe its independence.

Dan shall judge his people as one of the tribes of Israel. Dan shall be a serpent by the way, an adder in the path that bites the horse's heels. I have waited for thy salvation, O LORD. The old man pauses mid-blessing to pray. Dying does not stop intercession.

Dan shall judge his people as one of the tribes of Israel. Dan shall be a serpent by the way, an adder in the path that bites the horse's heels. I have waited for thy salvation, O LORD. The old man pauses mid-blessing to pray. Dying does not stop intercession.

Dan shall judge his people as one of the tribes of Israel. Dan shall be a serpent by the way, an adder in the path that bites the horse's heels. I have waited for thy salvation, O LORD. The old man pauses mid-blessing to pray. Dying does not stop intercession.

Gad — a troop shall overcome him, but he shall overcome at the last. Israel's eastern border tribe is being told: you will be raided, but you will also bite back. Hebrew wordplay packed into one verse.
Out of Asher his bread shall be fat — he shall yield royal dainties. The northwest coast tribe gets food that feeds kings. Asher's name means happy; his blessing is hospitality.
Naphtali is a hind let loose — a doe set free, giving goodly words. The northern tribe is gifted with speed, freedom, and beautiful speech. Each blessing is exactly the shape that fits that tribe.

Joseph is a fruitful bough — a fruitful bough by a well, whose branches run over the wall. The archers sorely grieved him, shot at him, hated him — but his bow remained strong, his arms made firm by the hands of the mighty God of Jacob. The story of Joseph's life condensed into two verses of poetry.

Joseph is a fruitful bough — a fruitful bough by a well, whose branches run over the wall. The archers sorely grieved him, shot at him, hated him — but his bow remained strong, his arms made firm by the hands of the mighty God of Jacob. The story of Joseph's life condensed into two verses of poetry.

Joseph is a fruitful bough — a fruitful bough by a well, whose branches run over the wall. The archers sorely grieved him, shot at him, hated him — but his bow remained strong, his arms made firm by the hands of the mighty God of Jacob. The story of Joseph's life condensed into two verses of poetry.

From the God of your father — who will help you — from the Almighty, who will bless you with blessings of heaven above, of the deep that lies beneath, of the breasts and of the womb. The blessings of your father have prevailed above the blessings of my ancestors. They shall be on the head of Joseph, on the crown of him who was separate from his brothers.

From the God of your father — who will help you — from the Almighty, who will bless you with blessings of heaven above, of the deep that lies beneath, of the breasts and of the womb. The blessings of your father have prevailed above the blessings of my ancestors. They shall be on the head of Joseph, on the crown of him who was separate from his brothers.

Benjamin shall ravin as a wolf: in the morning he shall devour the prey, and at night he shall divide the spoil. The youngest will be a fierce warrior tribe — Saul, the first king, will come from here.

All these are the twelve tribes of Israel; this is what their father spoke to them, and he blessed them — every one according to his blessing, he blessed them. The text is careful: it was all blessing, even the hard parts.

He charges them: I am to be gathered to my people. Bury me with my fathers in the cave that is in the field of Ephron the Hittite — the cave of Machpelah, in the field Abraham bought as a burial possession. There Abraham and Sarah are buried, Isaac and Rebekah are buried, and there I buried Leah. The patriarch names the wife he had not always loved, and asks to be laid beside her.

There Abraham and Sarah are buried, Isaac and Rebekah are buried, and there I buried Leah. The patriarch names the wife he had not always loved, and asks to be laid beside her.

He charges them: I am to be gathered to my people. Bury me with my fathers in the cave that is in the field of Ephron the Hittite — the cave of Machpelah, in the field Abraham bought as a burial possession. There Abraham and Sarah are buried, Isaac and Rebekah are buried, and there I buried Leah. The patriarch names the wife he had not always loved, and asks to be laid beside her.

When Jacob ends his commands, he draws his feet into the bed, yields up his spirit, and is gathered to his people. The whole arc of Genesis — from Adam — reaches the same word for every faithful death: gathered.
